citations.narrative:
In the enclosure housing three owl monkeys, there is a wooden nest box that has a build up of grime and dark brown
material across the surface and on the metal latches.
Not maintaining appropriate levels of cleanliness in areas where non-human primates come in contact can result in an
excessive build-up of materials, which can increase the disease risk for the animals contained within. Per the regulations,
hard surfaces with which nonhuman primates come in contact must be spot-cleaned daily and sanitized in accordance
with § 3.84 of this subpart to prevent accumulation of excreta or disease hazards. If the species scent mark, the surfaces
must be sanitized or replaced at regular intervals as determined by the attending veterinarian in accordance with
generally accepted professional and husbandry practices.
To be corrected by April 10, 2026.

In the enclosure housing four red foxes, the enclosure housing three red foxes, and the enclosure housing two bat eared
foxes, the animals have excavated under the wire dig guard. This has led to gaps beneath the wires which could allow an
animal’s foot and leg to pass through and potentially become entrapped or broken.
In the enclosure housing seven raccoons, there is a solar fan that is attached to the wall where the animals can access it.
The wires for the fan have been severed and pulled into the enclosure by the raccoons.
In the enclosure housing two kinkajou, there are sharp points of wires that are sticking into the enclosure where the food
bowls are positioned. There is also a layer of plastic wrap that the kinkajou have pulled into the enclosure that has
evidence of chewing.
Enclosures that are not kept in good repair can result in injury from entrapment or sharp points. Access to inappropriate
items like electric wires or plastic can cause injury or illness if ingested. The facility must be constructed of such material
and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. The housing facilities shall be maintained in good repair to
protect the animals from injury and to contain the animals.
To be corrected by April 24, 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
n

narrative:
3.75(c)(3) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
In the enclosure housing three owl monkeys, there is a wooden nest box that has a build up of grime and dark brown 
material across the surface and on the metal latches.  
Not maintaining appropriate levels of cleanliness in areas where non-human primates come in contact can result in an 
excessive build-up of materials, which can increase the disease risk for the animals contained within. Per the regulations, 
hard surfaces with which nonhuman primates come in contact must be spot-cleaned daily and sanitized in accordance 
with § 3.84 of this subpart to prevent accumulation of excreta or disease hazards. If the species scent mark, the surfaces 
must be sanitized or replaced at regular intervals as determined by the attending veterinarian in accordance with 
generally accepted professional and husbandry practices. 
To be corrected by April 10, 2026. 
3.125(a) 
   
Facilities, general. 
In the enclosure housing four red foxes, the enclosure housing three red foxes, and the enclosure housing two bat eared 
foxes, the animals have excavated under the wire dig guard. This has led to gaps beneath the wires which could allow an 
animal’s foot and leg to pass through and potentially become entrapped or broken. 
In the enclosure housing seven raccoons, there is a solar fan that is attached to the wall where the animals can access it. 
The wires for the fan have been severed and pulled into the enclosure by the raccoons. 
In the enclosure housing two kinkajou, there are sharp points of wires that are sticking into the enclosure where the food 
bowls are positioned. There is also a layer of plastic wrap that the kinkajou have pulled into the enclosure that has 
evidence of chewing.  
Enclosures that are not kept in good repair can result in injury from entrapment or sharp points. Access to inappropriate 
items like electric wires or plastic can cause injury or illness if ingested. The facility must be constructed of such material 
and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. The housing facilities shall be maintained in good repair to 
protect the animals from injury and to contain the animals. 
To be corrected by April 24, 2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
